author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
title: Grace Establishes Free Will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Chapter 52 [XXX.]—Grace Establishes Free Will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Do we then by grace make void free will?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Nay, rather we establish free will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: For even as the law by faith, so free will by grace, is not made void, but established.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: For neither is the law fulfilled except by free will; but by the law is the knowledge of sin, by faith the acquisition of grace against sin, by grace the healing of the soul from the disease of sin, by the health of the soul freedom of will, by free will the love of righteousness, by love of righteousness the accomplishment of the law.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Accordingly, as the law is not made void, but is established through faith, since faith procures grace whereby the law is fulfilled; so free will is not made void through grace, but is established, since grace cures the will whereby righteousness is freely loved.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Now all the stages which I have here connected together in their successive links, have severally their proper voices in the sacred Scriptures.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: The law says: “Thou shall not covet.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Faith says: “Heal my soul, for I have sinned against Thee.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Grace says: “Behold, thou art made whole: sin no more, lest a worse thing come unto thee.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Health says: “O Lord my God, I cried unto Thee, and Thou hast healed me.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Free will says: “I will freely sacrifice unto Thee.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Love of righteousness says: “Transgressors told me pleasant tales, but not according to Thy law, O Lord.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: How is it then that miserable men dare to be proud, either of their free will, before they are freed, or of their own strength, if they have been freed?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: They do not observe that in the very mention of free will they pronounce the name of liberty.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: But “where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: If, therefore, they are the slaves of sin, why do they boast of free will?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: For by what a man is overcome, to the same is he delivered as a slave.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: But if they have been freed, why do they vaunt themselves as if it were by their own doing, and boast, as if they had not received?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Spirit and the Letter.
verses.text: Or are they free in such sort that they do not choose to have Him for their Lord who says to them: “Without me ye can do nothing;” and “If the Son shall make you free, ye shall be free indeed?”
